narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their early and later school grades, undergraduate GPA, LSAT scores, family income, and whether they plan full-time studies—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it calculated a 54.6% probability of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold that triggered the rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish level, signaling to the model a dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, though under the average 3.2, actually worked in your favor by suggesting some resilience compared to even lower performers. Your high family income bracket also helped nudge the prediction toward passing, as students from similar backgrounds tend to have advantages that aid success. Your Year 1 decile of 3 and LSAT score of 32 both slightly increased the failure risk, being lower than the usual 5.7 and 36.7 averages, while your full-time study plan offered a tiny boost since most successful students follow that path. Overall, your profile stands out as having stronger supports like family resources than many, but the later academic decile stood out as the dominant concern relative to peers who passed. This isn't a judgment on your potential—models like this highlight patterns, not destinies, especially for underrepresented women like you navigating systemic hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later grades through targeted prep or additional courses, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your unique strengths and perseverance. Many have turned similar predictions around by addressing these key areas—reach out to advisors for bar prep resources tailored to Black women in law, and reapply stronger. You've got this; your story is just beginning.
